name: Acropolis of Athens
summary: The Acropolis of Athens is an ancient citadel perched on a rocky outcrop above the city, containing the remains of several ancient buildings of great architectural and historical significance. The most famous is the Parthenon, a temple dedicated to the goddess Athena, built in the 5th century BC during the Golden Age of Athens. It stands as the most important surviving complex of ancient Greek architecture and a symbol of Western civilization.

timeline.title: Construction of the Parthenon Begins
timeline.description: Pericles initiates the construction of the Parthenon, designed by architects Ictinus and Callicrates under the supervision of the sculptor Phidias. The temple is built entirely of Pentelic marble and dedicated to Athena Parthenos, the patron goddess of Athens.

timeline.title: Parthenon Completed
timeline.description: The Parthenon is completed after 15 years of construction. It features 46 outer columns and houses a massive gold and ivory statue of Athena by Phidias. The temple becomes the centerpiece of the Acropolis and the crowning achievement of Classical Greek architecture.

timeline.title: Roman Conquest of Greece
timeline.description: Rome conquers Greece and the Acropolis comes under Roman control. While some Roman emperors sponsor repairs and new constructions, the monuments gradually fall into disuse as pagan worship declines with the rise of Christianity.

timeline.title: Ottoman Conquest
timeline.description: The Ottoman Empire captures Athens and the Acropolis is converted into a military garrison. The Parthenon is transformed into a mosque, and the Erechtheion becomes the governor's harem. The site remains under Ottoman control for nearly 400 years.

timeline.title: Venetian Bombardment and Explosion
timeline.description: During the Morean War, Venetian forces under Francesco Morosini bombard the Acropolis. A cannonball ignites the Ottoman gunpowder magazine stored inside the Parthenon, causing a catastrophic explosion that destroys much of the ancient temple and scatters sculptures across the hillside.

timeline.title: Elgin Removes the Marbles
timeline.description: British Lord Elgin obtains controversial permission from the Ottoman authorities to remove about half of the surviving Parthenon sculptures. These 'Elgin Marbles' are shipped to Britain and eventually sold to the British Museum, where they remain a subject of ongoing repatriation disputes.

timeline.title: Modern Restoration Begins
timeline.description: Greece launches a comprehensive restoration program for the Acropolis monuments using original materials and ancient techniques. The project, overseen by the Committee for the Conservation of the Acropolis Monuments, continues today and is one of the most ambitious archaeological restorations in history.

images.caption: The newly completed Parthenon in all its Classical glory, adorned with vivid paint and sculptures
The Acropolis under Ottoman rule with minarets added to the ancient structures
The devastating explosion of the Parthenon during the Venetian bombardment
The restored Acropolis standing proudly above modern Athens at sunset
